title_en: The Definition of Bid'ah and Its Place in Islamic Law
title_ar: تعريف البدعة وحكمها في الشريعة الإسلامية
content_en:
Al-I'tisam (Holding Fast to the Book and Sunnah) is one of the most significant works in the Islamic scholarly tradition on the topic of religious innovation (bid'ah). Its author, Abu Ishaq Ibrahim ibn Musa ibn Muhammad al-Lakhmi al-Gharnati al-Maliki, known as al-Shatibi (d. 790 AH / 1388 CE), was a distinguished scholar of Maliki jurisprudence and the philosophy of Islamic law in Andalusia. His Al-Muwafaqat, a masterwork on the objectives of Islamic law (maqasid al-shariah), is his other great contribution, but Al-I'tisam is equally important for its systematic treatment of the principle that the Islamic religion was completed at the death of the Prophet and that introducing new religious practices into it is a fundamental error.

The word 'bid'ah' in Arabic derives from the root b-d-', meaning to create or originate something without precedent. In its Quranic usage, the term carries this neutral meaning: 'I am not an originator (bid') among the messengers' (46:9) — meaning the Prophet was not the first messenger but one in a long line. The specific theological meaning of bid'ah — a religious innovation that has no basis in the Quran or Sunnah — was established by the hadith scholars and jurists who recognized that the Prophet had warned against it: 'Every newly invented thing is a bid'ah, and every bid'ah is going astray, and every going astray is in the Fire.'

Al-Shatibi's treatment of bid'ah in Al-I'tisam is notable for its philosophical depth. He does not simply compile hadiths condemning innovation but builds a systematic argument for why religious innovation is fundamentally incompatible with the nature of the Islamic Sharia. His argument rests on several pillars. First, the Quran explicitly states that the religion was completed: 'This day I have perfected your religion for you and completed My favor upon you and have approved for you Islam as religion' (5:3). If the religion is perfect and complete, any addition to it is an implicit claim that the Prophet failed to convey something necessary — a claim that contradicts the Quranic assurance of completeness.

Second, al-Shatibi argues that the purpose of religious law (maqsad al-shari') is not merely to command specific acts but to establish a comprehensive way of life that conforms to divine guidance. When a person introduces a religious innovation — a new form of worship, a new religious observance, a new ritual — they are in effect substituting their own judgment for divine guidance on how to worship Allah. This is a form of following one's desires (hawa) in religious matters, which the Quran condemns repeatedly. The innovator implicitly says, 'I know better than the Prophet how to worship Allah,' which is a form of religious arrogance that the Islamic tradition consistently criticizes.

Third, al-Shatibi examines the Companion generation's attitude toward religious innovation as evidence for the correct interpretation of the prophetic hadith. The Companions were scrupulous in following the Prophet's practice and resistant to introducing new forms of worship. 'Umar ibn al-Khattab, when he organized the Tarawih prayers into a congregational format during Ramadan, reportedly said 'What a good bid'ah this is!' — a statement that al-Shatibi and later scholars analyze carefully. The majority interpretation is that 'Umar used 'bid'ah' in its linguistic sense of 'something new,' not in its technical religious sense of 'an innovation in religion,' since the Tarawih prayer itself had explicit prophetic precedent; 'Umar was merely organizing its collective performance.

Al-Shatibi distinguishes between religious bid'ah (bid'ah diniyya) — innovations in the practice of worship and religious observance — and worldly innovations (bid'at al-'ada) — new developments in everyday life, technology, and social customs. The prohibition of bid'ah applies specifically to religious matters: inventing new forms of prayer, new religious holidays, new rituals, new obligations. It does not prohibit all novelty in human life. This distinction was important in al-Shatibi's time, when some scholars had expanded the concept of bid'ah so broadly as to condemn any historical development, while others had narrowed it so much as to render it practically meaningless. Al-I'tisam represents a careful recovery of the middle position grounded in the principles of Islamic jurisprudence.
content_ar: البدعة في الاصطلاح طريقة في الدين مخترعة تضاهي الشرعية يُقصد بالسلوك عليها المبالغة في التعبد لله سبحانه.
